senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to the sturgeon family of fishes.
examples: The researchers examined several acipenserid species in the river.
Acipenserid populations have declined because of overfishing.

senses.definition: A fish of the sturgeon family Acipenseridae.
examples: The lake is home to an endangered acipenserid.
An acipenserid can live for many decades in the wild.

senses.definition: A family of large, mainly bottom‑feeding fishes that includes the true sturgeons.
examples: Acipenseridae are found in rivers and coastal waters of the northern hemisphere.
Many members of Acipenseridae migrate upriver to spawn.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of sturgeons.
examples: The river supports several acipenserine fishes.
Acipenserine traits were noted in the fossil record.

senses.definition: Belonging to or resembling the sturgeons and paddlefishes of the order Acipenseriformes.
examples: The larvae of acipenseroid fishes have a distinct feeding stage.
Acipenseroid species are considered ancient ray‑finned fishes.

senses.definition: A fish of the group that includes sturgeons and paddlefishes.
examples: The acipenseroid is adapted to life near the riverbed.
Fossil acipenseroids help scientists understand fish evolution.

senses.definition: A suborder of ray‑finned fishes that includes sturgeons and paddlefishes.
examples: Acipenseroidei comprise both living and extinct sturgeons and paddlefishes.
The suborder Acipenseroidei is of special interest to evolutionary biologists.

senses.definition: In classical mythology, a Sicilian shepherd loved by the sea nymph Galatea and killed by the giant Polyphemus.
examples: In the myth, Acis is crushed by a rock thrown by Polyphemus.
Painters often showed Galatea mourning Acis.

senses.definition: Surgery, especially as a branch of medical science. (rare)
examples: Nineteenth‑century texts sometimes refer to surgery as aciurgy.
He collected old manuals on aciurgy and other medical arts.

senses.definition: Used to show sudden dislike, disgust, or annoyance.
examples: Ack, this milk has gone bad.
Ack, I forgot to send the email.

senses.definition: A tropical tree of West Africa and the Caribbean, or its red fruit whose yellow flesh is eaten when fully ripe.
examples: Ackee is a key ingredient in Jamaica's national dish.
Only the ripe yellow parts of the ackee are safe to eat.

senses.definition: Plural of ackee.
examples: The market stall was piled high with fresh ackees.
She cooked the ackees with salted cod and peppers.

senses.definition: A person who works on or owns fields or farmland. (dialect or historical)
examples: The old acker spent his days ploughing the fields.
As an acker, she knew every corner of the valley.

senses.definition: Money, especially small amounts of cash. (British slang)
examples: I don't have the ackers to go out tonight.
He earned a few ackers doing odd jobs.

senses.definition: A former gold coin of the Gold Coast in West Africa, later used as a money unit in Ghana.
examples: Traders were paid in ackey and other local coins.
The museum displays an original ackey from the eighteenth century.

senses.definition: Plural of ackey, old coins or monetary units formerly used in West Africa.
examples: He collected several worn ackeys from the colonial period.
The payment was counted in ackeys and cowrie shells.

senses.definition: A person whose family name is Ackman.
examples: Mr Ackman introduced himself simply as Ackman.
She said that Ackman would handle the negotiations.

senses.definition: Plural of Ackman, referring to people with the surname Ackman.
examples: The Ackmen were well known in the town.
Several Ackmen attended the family reunion.

senses.definition: Old or dialect past form of acknowledge, meaning accepted or admitted something. (rare)
examples: He acknew his fault before the council.
At last the king acknew their loyalty.

senses.definition: A shortened or dialect form of acknowledge, meaning to accept or admit that something is true. (non‑standard)
examples: I must acknow my share of the blame.
They would not acknow the risk.

senses.definition: Non‑standard or obsolete form of acknowledging, meaning accepting or admitting something.
examples: By acknowing his errors, he began to change.
She wrote a letter acknowing their support.

senses.definition: To accept or admit that something is true or real.
examples: He finally acknowledged his mistake.
We must acknowledge that the plan has failed.

senses.definition: To show that you have received something such as a letter, message, or payment.
examples: Please acknowledge receipt of this email.
The company acknowledged the order by return of post.

senses.definition: To publicly show thanks or recognition for help or support.
examples: She acknowledged her team's hard work in her speech.
We would like to acknowledge the support of our sponsors.

senses.definition: Able or fit to be acknowledged or admitted.
examples: There was an acknowledgeable difference between the two versions.
Only acknowledgeable debts were listed in the report.

senses.definition: In a way that is openly or clearly acknowledged.
examples: He is acknowledgeably one of the best writers of his generation.
The project was acknowledgeably difficult from the start.

senses.definition: Generally accepted or recognized as true, important, or good.
examples: She is an acknowledged expert in her field.
The book is an acknowledged classic.

senses.definition: Past simple or past participle of acknowledge: accepted or admitted something.
examples: He acknowledged he had made a serious error.
They acknowledged the importance of cooperation.

senses.definition: As is generally accepted or recognized.
examples: He is acknowledgedly one of the finest actors of his time.
The task was acknowledgedly more complex than expected.

senses.definition: The act of accepting or admitting that something is true or exists.
examples: There is growing acknowledgement that climate change is real.
His nod was a quiet acknowledgement of her help.

senses.definition: Something given or said to thank someone for help or support.
examples: The author wrote a warm acknowledgement to her family.
Please find enclosed a small gift in acknowledgement of your kindness.

senses.definition: A letter, note, or message that shows something has been received.
examples: We received an acknowledgement from the bank.
He sent an acknowledgement of the order the same day.

senses.definition: A section in a book or document where the writer thanks people for their help and support.
examples: She mentioned her former teachers in the acknowledgements.
The acknowledgements appear at the end of the thesis.

senses.definition: Letters or notes that confirm something has been received.
examples: We are still waiting for the final acknowledgements from our partners.
Several acknowledgements of the shipment arrived today.

senses.definition: A person who acknowledges or admits something. (rare)
examples: He was an eager acknowledger of other people's achievements.
As an acknowledger of his own faults, he was easy to work with.

senses.definition: People who acknowledge or admit something. (rare)
examples: They became open acknowledgers of their own biases.
The acknowledgers of the mistake apologised publicly.

senses.definition: Third person singular of acknowledge: accepts or admits that something is true.
examples: She acknowledges that she may be wrong.
The report acknowledges the need for change.

senses.definition: Present participle of acknowledge: accepting or admitting something, or showing thanks for it.
examples: By acknowledging his errors, he built trust.
She is acknowledging the team's support in her speech.

senses.definition: The act of accepting or admitting that something is true or exists. (US spelling)
examples: There was a clear acknowledgment of past mistakes.
Her smile was an acknowledgment that she understood.

senses.definition: Something given or said to thank someone for help or support. (US spelling)
examples: The book includes an acknowledgment of her colleagues.
They sent a small gift in acknowledgment of his efforts.

senses.definition: A letter, note, or message that shows that something has been received. (US spelling)
examples: We received an acknowledgment from the editor.
Please send an acknowledgment when the package arrives.

senses.definition: A section in a book or document where the writer thanks people for their help and support. (US spelling)
examples: He mentioned his family in the acknowledgments.
The acknowledgments are printed at the front of the book.

senses.definition: Letters or notes that confirm something has been received. (US spelling)
examples: We are waiting for the final acknowledgments from our clients.
Several acknowledgments of the payment arrived by email.

senses.definition: Old or dialect past participle of acknowledge, meaning admitted or recognized. (rare)
examples: His guilt was long acknown among his friends.
The treaty was acknown by both parties.

senses.definition: Obsolete form related to acknowledge, meaning to admit or recognize. (very rare)
examples: He would not acknowne the charge laid against him.
The debt was at last acknowne by the merchant.

senses.definition: Non‑standard or dialect third person singular of acknow, meaning acknowledges.
examples: He acknows his part in the failure.
She finally acknows that they were right.

senses.definition: The name of a place or family; used mainly as a proper name.
examples: She grew up in a small village called Ackton.
The Ackton estate lay beyond the river.

senses.definition: Not refracting or bending light; having no refraction. (technical)
examples: The mineral was described as aclastic under the microscope.
An aclastic medium does not change the direction of light.

senses.definition: A tropical timber tree (Aegiceras or related genera) used for wood in some regions. (regional)
examples: The villagers cut acle for building small boats.
Acle wood is valued for its strength.

senses.definition: Relating to or resembling the clavicle (collarbone). (rare anatomical term)
examples: The fossil showed acleidian features in the shoulder girdle.
Acleidian structures developed early in the species.

senses.definition: A congenital condition in which the heart is not fully closed or formed. (technical, rare)
examples: The infant was diagnosed with acleistocardia soon after birth.
Acleistocardia is usually detected by detailed imaging.

senses.definition: Not closed; remaining open. (technical, especially in biology or anatomy)
examples: Certain acleistous structures allow continuous flow between chambers.
The species has an acleistous valve at this stage.

senses.definition: A very poor or wretched person. (obsolete)
examples: The tale tells of an aclemon wandering from town to town.
She gave a coin to the aclemon at the gate.

senses.definition: Relating to the axis or midline of the body. (very rare, technical)
examples: The organism shows aclidian symmetry.
Several aclidian features appear in the early embryo.

senses.definition: Not inclined; without a slope or dip. (geology)
examples: An aclinal fold shows no overall dip of the rock layers.
The region is largely aclinal and structurally simple.

senses.definition: Having zero magnetic inclination; parallel to the earth's surface. (geophysics)
examples: The magnetic field is aclinic along this special line.
Aclinic points mark where the needle stays level.

senses.definition: In a cloud; in a cloudy or darkened state. (poetic or dialect)
examples: The hills lay acloud in the morning mist.
His thoughts were all acloud with worry.

senses.definition: Short for the American Civil Liberties Union, an organization that works to protect individual rights and freedoms in the United States.
examples: The ACLU filed a lawsuit challenging the new law.
She decided to donate to the ACLU after reading about the case.

senses.definition: Plural of aclys, a small Roman javelin. (historical)
examples: The soldiers carried their aclydes into battle.
Several aclydes were found at the dig site.

senses.definition: A small Roman javelin or spear, often with a strap for throwing. (historical)
examples: Each warrior gripped an aclys in his hand.
The aclys was designed for quick, accurate throws.

senses.definition: A genus of small marine snails or limpets found on rocky shores. (zoology)
examples: Acmaea are common on the exposed rocks at low tide.
The biologist studied several species of Acmaea.
